content.problemStatement:
An entire class of students has gone to the ATM to withdraw cash. Let's name them {S1,S2,…,SN}\{S_1, S_2, \dots, S_N\}{S1​,S2​,…,SN​}. Obviously, they have to stand in a queue. They want to do so, such that close friends are standing not too far from each other. Specifically, if SiS_iSi​ and SjS_jSj​ are close friends, then there should not be more than 2 other students standing in between them. Given the close-friendship information, you need to tell whether it is possible to form such a queue, or not.

Formally, you should state whether there exists a permutation (Sp1,Sp2,…,SpN)(S_{p1}, S_{p2}, \dots, S_{pN})(Sp1​,Sp2​,…,SpN​) of {S1,S2,…,SN}\{S_1, S_2, \dots, S_N\}{S1​,S2​,…,SN​}, such that for all pairs (Spi,Spj)(S_{pi}, S_{pj})(Spi​,Spj​) where SpiS_{pi}Spi​ and SpjS_{pj}Spj​ are close friends, ∣i−j∣≤3|i-j| \leq 3∣i−j∣≤3 holds true.


Input Format
The first line of input contains an integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.


The first line contains two integers N,MN, MN,M, denoting the number of students, and the number of close-friendship pairs, respectively.


The next MMM lines contain two integers each, separated by single spaces: i ji\ ji j, denoting that SiS_iSi​ and SjS_jSj​ are close friends.



Output Format
Output a single line for each test case, containing the answer, which should be 111, if such a queue is possible, and 000 otherwise.
content.inputFormat: The first line of input contains an integer T denoting the number of test cases. The description of T test cases follows. The first line contains two integers N, M, denoting the number of students and the number of close-friendship pairs, respectively. The next M lines contain two integers each, separated by single spaces: i j, denoting that Si and Sj are close friends.
content.outputFormat: Output a single line for each test case, containing the answer, which should be 1 if such a queue is possible, and 0 otherwise.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Consider the permutation (S1, S3, S2, S4, S5). No pair of close friends have more than 2 students between them. So this is a valid queue, and hence the answer is 1.
There is no valid permutation where all pairs of close friends have at most 2 students between them.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a graph coloring approach. We create a graph where each student is a node, and two nodes are connected if the corresponding students are close friends. We then try to color the graph using 4 colors (since each student can be at most 3 positions away from their close friends). If the graph can be colored, then it is possible to form a queue where close friends are not too far apart. Otherwise, it is not possible.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * (N + M)) — where T is the number of test cases, N is the number of students, and M is the number of close-friendship pairs. The time complexity is dominated by the graph coloring algorithm.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N + M) — where N is the number of students and M is the number of close-friendship pairs. The space complexity is dominated by the graph data structure.
